steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage describes how Chinese immigrants from the north (Mandarin speakers) settled in Queens, while those from the south (Cantonese/Fuzhounese speakers) settled in Manhattan — mirroring the regional language geography of China. The question asks what can be inferred from this pattern.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — languages tend to change more rapidly in areas where many languages are spoken than in areas where few languages are spoken.: The passage says nothing about how quickly languages change. ✗
  • Choice B — correlations in a country between languages and regions where they are spoken can replicate themselves in a new country to which the original country's citizens emigrate.: This is exactly what the passage shows — the north/south linguistic split from China is reproduced in New York. ✓
  • Choice C — languages spoken by immigrant peoples can differ significantly in vocabulary and pronunciation from those same languages in their country of origin.: The passage discusses different languages (Mandarin vs. Cantonese), not variation within a single language. ✗
  • Choice D — there is a positive correlation between the physical size of a country and the number of languages spoken in that country.: Country size is never mentioned. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
